narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at key aspects of your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 29, and your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3—both at the lowest 10th decile, far below the typical student's mid-range performance around the 5th or 6th decile. It also noted your plan to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of students. Overall, the model predicted an 88.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, a risk level that led to the rejection of your application to protect the school's outcomes and resources. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking in the bottom 10%, as it strongly signals weaker performance relative to peers who typically succeed. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom 10%, which reinforced concerns about consistent academic struggles from early on. Interestingly, your undergraduate GPA provided a slight counterbalance, nudging the prediction a bit toward passing since it's not drastically below the average of 3.21, though it wasn't enough to offset the other red flags. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have stronger early and later school rankings closer to the middle or top deciles, your profile stood out as higher risk, even with your full-time commitment aligning with the majority. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar family incomes succeed with better academic markers—but the model prioritizes patterns from past bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average of 36.7, strengthening your high school decile performance through additional coursework or programs, and perhaps gaining relevant experience to rebuild your academic narrative. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors at schools that value growth stories like yours.
